title: Modular Bar Wine Grid Hutch
summary: A hutch unit for a modular bar system, built around a removable interlocking plywood wine grid that slides out through the front.
description:
This hutch is designed to sit on top of a matching modular base, and its sides are ripped from 3/4" plywood into 11-1/2"-wide strips rather than built from solid 1x12 — plywood strips stay flatter over time and let you share material with the base if you're building both. If you'd rather use real 1x12 lumber, that works too, but measure your actual board width first since it will shift the other measurements slightly.

The wine grid itself is built from interlocking 1/4" plywood dividers — three tall pieces and seven short ones, slid together like a puzzle rather than glued into a fixed frame. Clamping the pieces together and cutting them as a stack, rather than one at a time, is what keeps every slot lining up. The grid is built to lift out through the front for cleaning or rearranging, and the whole hutch must be secured to the base below it and to a wall stud behind — a tall, front-loaded cabinet like this is a genuine tipping hazard if left to stand on its own.

tools.note: Rips the 3/4" plywood into 11-1/2" strips and cuts the stacked wine-grid dividers.
Clamps the wine-grid dividers together for a stacked cut.

materials.name: 3/4" plywood, half sheet, ripped into 11-1/2" strips
materials.note: Or substitute solid 1x12 — measure your actual board width and adjust measurements.

cutList.note: Clamp all three together and cut as a stack for identical slots.
Clamp together and cut as a stack.

steps.title: Cut the carcass parts
steps.body: Rip the 3/4" plywood into 11 1/2"-wide strips, then crosscut two sides 34", the shelf 14 3/4", and the top 17 3/4". From the 2x2 cut four legs 34" — the same length as the sides, so their tops and bottoms will finish flush. From the 1x2 cut four side trim pieces 11 1/2", three shelf trim and top support pieces 14 3/4", and one top trim 17 3/4"; cut the 17 3/4" top front trim from the 1x3. Buy two 8 ft 1x2s rather than the one listed: those eight pieces come to 108" and a single 8 ft board is only 96". Cut the 1/4" plywood back 17 3/4" x 34 3/4" now, but leave the wine-grid dividers uncut until the carcass is assembled and measured.
materials: 3/4" plywood, half sheet, ripped into 11-1/2" strips

steps.title: Drill pocket holes and trim the sides
steps.body:
This project is shown built from 3/4" plywood ripped into 11-1/2"-wide strips; you can use solid 1x12 instead, but measure your actual board width first and adjust measurements accordingly.

Drill pocket holes along the side and top edges of both side pieces for attaching the top and legs in later steps. Then, on the outside faces, nail 1x2 trim at the top and bottom with 1-1/4" finish nails and glue, keeping the outside edges flush.
materials: 3/4" plywood, half sheet, ripped into 11-1/2" strips

steps.title: Attach the legs
steps.body: Stand a 34" 2x2 leg at each front and back corner of both sides, outside faces flush with the side panel's front and back edges, and fasten through the pocket holes you drilled with 1 1/4" screws and glue. Because the legs and the sides are both 34", their tops and bottoms should finish flush — clamp each leg to its panel and check that before driving a screw. A leg set even a sixteenth proud will hold the top off the carcass and show as a gap along the whole front.

steps.title: Build and attach the bottom shelf
steps.body: Drill pocket holes in both ends of the 14 3/4" shelf and fasten it between the sides, flush to the bottom, with 1 1/4" pocket-hole screws and glue, then nail its 1x2 trim across the front. That 14 3/4" shelf length is also the length of every short wine-grid divider — measure the finished opening once the shelf is in and write it down, because the grid has to slide into that number, not into the drawing's.
materials: 3/4" plywood, half sheet, ripped into 11-1/2" strips

steps.title: Attach the top
steps.body: Set the 17 3/4" top across the carcass flush to the front and fasten it down through the pocket holes in the top edges of the sides. Nail the 17 3/4" 1x2 top trim and the 17 3/4" 1x3 top front trim on with 1 1/4" finish nails and glue, keeping the outside edges flush so the front reads as a single thickness rather than a stack. Wipe the squeeze-out off now — glue left in a corner will refuse stain later.
materials: 3/4" plywood, half sheet, ripped into 11-1/2" strips

steps.title: Dry-fit and check square
steps.body: Before cutting the wine grid, dry-fit the carcass together and check it for square — the grid pieces below need a true rectangular opening to slide into.

steps.title: Cut the tall wine-grid dividers
steps.body: Stack the three tall divider blanks — 13 3/4" wide by 33 1/4" long — clamp them together with bar clamps, and cut every interlocking slot through the stack at once so all three land in identical places. Cut the slots a hair wider than 1/4": nominal 1/4" plywood is rarely a true quarter inch, and a slot cut exactly to the nominal size will jam rather than slide. Test one slot on an offcut before committing to the stack. At 33 1/4" the tall dividers stand clear of the 34" sides, which is what lets the assembled grid lift back out.

steps.title: Cut the short wine-grid dividers
steps.body: Cut the seven short dividers 14 3/4" long by 13 3/4" wide, clamped as a stack the same way, with their slots spaced to match the tall dividers'. Cut these slots in from the opposite edge — one set slots down and the other up, and that opposition is the whole mechanism of the interlock. Dry-fit one tall and one short piece together before you cut the slots in the rest of the stack.

steps.title: Assemble the wine grid
steps.body: Slide the tall and short dividers together on a flat surface, working from one end so each joint seats fully before you add the next. Leave the grid completely dry — no glue and no fasteners — because coming apart is the entire point of the interlocking joint. Check that the assembled grid measures no more than the opening you recorded earlier, and ease the slots with sandpaper if anything is tight rather than forcing it home.

steps.title: Install the wine grid
steps.body: Slide the assembled grid into the hutch through the front. Keep the small shelf pieces removable from the front so you can later pull the shelves out, collapse the tall dividers, and lift the whole grid back out for cleaning or rearranging. Load a bottle into each of several slots before anything is fastened permanently — a wine bottle is longer and heavier than it looks on paper, and a slot that will not take one is far easier to open up now than after the back is on.

steps.title: Attach the back and secure the hutch
steps.body: Nail the back panel on with glue. Secure the hutch to its base to avoid a tipping hazard, and also screw it to a wall stud behind it — this is a tall, front-loaded piece and must not be left freestanding.

images.alt: Modular bar hutch with a removable interlocking wine grid
